IS.q: Which of the following is a control variable in an experiment?
options: The amount of light you change to measure plant growth
The height of the plant that you measure
The type of soil you keep the same for all plants
The time period over which you collect data
IS.explanation: A control variable is kept the same throughout an experiment to ensure a fair test. The soil type is kept constant while other factors change.
IS.hint: Think 'Control' = keeping things the same to make it fair.

IS.q: A student measures the time it takes for sugar to dissolve in water at different temperatures. What is the independent variable?
options: The time taken for sugar to dissolve
IS.explanation: The independent variable is what you deliberately change. The student is changing the temperature to see how it affects dissolving time.
IS.hint: Ask yourself: 'What am I deliberately changing?' That is your independent variable.

IS.q: What is the name of the variable you measure or observe as a result of your experiment?
IS.explanation: The dependent variable is what you measure - it is the effect or result that depends on what you changed (the independent variable).
IS.hint: It 'depends on' the independent variable. It is the result you measure.

IS.q: Which type of data is the colour of a flower?
IS.explanation: Categoric data is descriptive - it is a label or category (like colours, plant species, or names). It is not a number.
IS.hint: Can you count the colours on a scale, or are they just different types/categories?

IS.q: Which of the following is an example of discrete data?
options: The height of a student in cm
The number of leaves on a plant
The mass of a rock in grams
IS.explanation: Discrete data can only have whole number values - you cannot have 3.7 leaves. The other options are continuous (can have decimal values).
IS.hint: Think about whether you could have a decimal/fraction of the measurement.

IS.q: Which of the following is a control variable in an experiment?
options: The thing you keep the same
IS.explanation: Control variables are kept the same throughout an experiment to ensure a fair test. They prevent other factors from affecting your results.
IS.hint: Think about what you need to keep the same to make an experiment fair.

IS.q: What is a hypothesis in science?
options: A prediction based on scientific knowledge
IS.explanation: A hypothesis is an educated guess or prediction that can be tested by an experiment. It is not yet proven.
IS.hint: It's what you think will happen BEFORE you do the experiment.

IS.q: Which type of data is 'colour of a flower'?
IS.explanation: Categoric data consists of words or labels, like colours, names, or types. It cannot be measured numerically.
IS.hint: Is this a word/label or a number you can measure?

IS.q: What does it mean to replicate an experiment?
options: To do the experiment multiple times
IS.explanation: Replication means repeating an experiment several times to check that your results are reliable and not due to chance.
IS.hint: Think about doing the same thing over and over again.

IS.q: When presenting results, what is the main purpose of drawing a graph?
options: To make your work look neater
To show a pattern or trend in the data
To use up space in your report
IS.explanation: Graphs help visualise data and make it easier to see patterns, trends, or relationships between variables.
IS.hint: Think about what a graph makes easier to see compared to a table of numbers.

IS.q: What is an anomalous result?
options: A result that doesn't follow the pattern
A result that is very large
IS.explanation: An anomalous result is one that doesn't fit the pattern of your other results. It may be due to experimental error or an unexpected event.
IS.hint: It's the odd one out that doesn't fit the pattern.

IS.q: What is a hypothesis?
options: A final conclusion after an experiment
A testable prediction based on scientific knowledge
A list of equipment needed for an experiment
The method you use to collect data
IS.explanation: A hypothesis is an educated guess or prediction that can be tested through experimentation. It is based on existing scientific knowledge.
IS.hint: It's a prediction that you can test — think 'I think... because...'.

IS.q: A student measures the temperature of boiling water five times and records: 98°C, 99°C, 100°C, 100°C, 101°C. What is the mean temperature?
IS.hint: Add all five values together, then divide by 5.

IS.q: Which piece of equipment would be most suitable for measuring exactly 25 mL of liquid in a chemistry experiment?
IS.explanation: A pipette or burette measures liquids very precisely. A measuring cylinder is less precise. Beakers are for rough estimates only.
IS.hint: Which equipment is designed for precise volume measurement?

IS.q: Which of the following is an example of a qualitative observation?
options: The mass decreased from 10 g to 8 g
The volume of gas was 250 mL
IS.explanation: A qualitative observation describes a quality or characteristic (colour, smell, texture) without numbers. The other options are quantitative—they use measurements.
IS.hint: Qualitative = descriptions without numbers. Which doesn't have a number in it?

IS.q: A student repeats a mass measurement four times and gets: 25.0 g, 25.1 g, 25.0 g, 25.2 g. The actual mass is 35.0 g. Which statement is true?
options: The results are accurate but not precise
The results are precise but not accurate
The results are both accurate and precise
The results are neither accurate nor precise
IS.explanation: Results are close together (25.0–25.2 g) so they are precise. But they are far from 35.0 g so they are not accurate—there is a systematic error.
IS.hint: Check if readings are close to each other AND close to the true value.

IS.q: Why is it important to use appropriate units when recording scientific measurements?
options: It makes the work look neater
It ensures others can understand and interpret your results correctly
It reduces the need for repeats
It automatically makes the experiment more accurate
IS.explanation: Units (g, cm, °C, mL, etc.) communicate the type and scale of measurement. Without them, results are meaningless and others cannot compare or use your data.
IS.hint: What would '25' mean without knowing if it's grams, kilograms, or millilitres?

IS.q: A thermometer reads 23°C, 23°C, 23°C, and 45°C. What is the most likely explanation for the 45°C reading?
options: It is an anomalous result caused by random error
Thermometers always give one incorrect reading
IS.explanation: The 45°C reading is anomalous—it doesn't fit the pattern of the other three readings and should be ignored when calculating the mean.
IS.hint: Which reading is very different from the others?

IS.q: In an experiment testing how temperature affects the speed of a reaction, which is the independent variable?
IS.explanation: Temperature is the variable being deliberately changed by the scientist, making it the independent variable.
IS.hint: Which variable does the scientist change intentionally?

IS.q: Which type of error can be reduced by repeating an experiment and calculating a mean?
IS.explanation: Random errors vary unpredictably; repeating measurements and averaging helps cancel out these random variations. Systematic errors affect all readings in the same way.
IS.hint: Which error occurs randomly and differently each time?

IS.q: A student wants to investigate how the amount of salt affects the boiling point of water. Which of the following should be kept constant?
IS.explanation: The volume of water and starting temperature must both be kept constant (control variables) so that only the salt amount (independent variable) affects the boiling point (dependent variable).
IS.hint: What factors other than salt could affect boiling point?

IS.q: A digital balance reads 45.67 g for the same object each time it is weighed. This measurement is:
IS.explanation: The readings are consistent (precise—to 2 decimal places) and, assuming 45.67 g is the true value, they are also close to the correct value (accurate).
IS.hint: Are the readings close together and close to the true value?

CS.q: What is the main function of the cell membrane?
options: To control what enters and leaves the cell
CS.explanation: The cell membrane controls which substances can enter and leave the cell.
CS.hint: Think about what acts as a barrier or gatekeeper.

CS.q: Which of these is a unicellular organism?
CS.explanation: Bacteria are single-celled organisms. The others are multicellular.
CS.hint: Which one is made of just one cell?

CS.q: What do ribosomes do in a cell?
CS.explanation: Ribosomes are the sites where proteins are manufactured in the cell.
CS.hint: Think about what builds things in the cell.

CS.q: How many cells does a bacteria cell have?
CS.explanation: Bacteria are unicellular organisms - they consist of just one cell.
CS.hint: The prefix 'uni-' means one.

CS.q: Which organelle contains genetic information in plant and animal cells?
CS.explanation: The nucleus contains DNA, which carries all genetic information needed to control the cell.
CS.hint: Think of it as the cell's instruction manual.

CS.q: Cells become specialised to perform specific functions. What is this process called?
CS.explanation: Differentiation is when a cell changes to become specialised for a particular job (e.g., nerve cells, muscle cells).
CS.hint: It's when cells change to fit different jobs.

CS.q: What is the main function of the cell membrane?
options: To control what enters and leaves the cell
CS.explanation: The cell membrane is a barrier that controls which substances can enter and exit the cell.
CS.hint: Think about what acts like a gate or filter for the cell.

CS.q: Which of these is a function of the cell wall?
CS.explanation: The cell wall is a rigid structure around plant cells that provides structural support and protection.
CS.hint: It's only found in plant cells and helps them stay rigid and strong.

CS.q: What does the cytoplasm do?
options: Contains cell fluid where reactions occur
CS.explanation: The cytoplasm is a gel-like substance that fills the cell and contains dissolved nutrients where many chemical reactions take place.
CS.hint: It's the 'jelly' inside the cell where things happen.

CS.q: Which organelle is responsible for breaking down waste materials in cells?
CS.explanation: Lysosomes contain digestive enzymes that break down and dispose of waste materials within the cell.
CS.hint: The name sounds like 'lysis', which means breaking down.

CS.q: In plant cells, the vacuole is mainly used for:
options: Storing water and maintaining cell shape
CS.explanation: The large central vacuole in plant cells stores water, nutrients, and helps the plant cell maintain its rigid shape and firmness.
CS.hint: It keeps the plant cell firm and stores cell sap.

CS.q: Which statement about ribosomes is correct?
options: They only exist in plant cells
They make proteins for the cell
CS.explanation: Ribosomes are found in all cells and are responsible for synthesizing (making) proteins based on instructions from the nucleus.
CS.hint: Think about what small structures make proteins in cells.

CS.q: A red blood cell is specialised to carry oxygen. Which feature helps it do this job most effectively?
options: It has a thick cell wall
CS.explanation: Haemoglobin is a protein in red blood cells that binds to oxygen and transports it around the body.
CS.hint: Think about what substance red blood cells need to carry.

CS.q: Which of the following is the correct order of organisation in living organisms, from smallest to largest?
CS.explanation: The correct hierarchy is: cells combine to form tissues, tissues form organs, organs form systems, and systems make up the organism.
CS.hint: Start with the smallest living unit.

CS.q: A pancreas cell produces digestive enzymes. Which organelle would this cell contain in large numbers?
CS.explanation: Ribosomes are where proteins (including enzymes) are made. A cell that produces many enzymes needs many ribosomes.
CS.hint: Where in the cell are proteins manufactured?

CS.q: Why do cells that line the small intestine have many finger-like projections called villi?
options: To increase surface area for absorption of nutrients
To help the intestine move food along
CS.explanation: The villi greatly increase the surface area of the epithelial tissue, allowing more efficient absorption of digested nutrients.
CS.hint: What advantage does a larger surface area give for absorption?

CS.q: A sperm cell is specialised for fertilisation. Which two features help it reach and fertilise an egg?
options: Flagellum for movement and acrosome with enzymes to penetrate egg
CS.explanation: The flagellum (tail) provides movement, while the acrosome releases enzymes that help the sperm penetrate the egg cell membrane.
CS.hint: Think about what the sperm cell needs to do: move quickly and break through the egg.

CS.q: A nerve cell has a very long extension called an axon. Why is this adaptation useful?
options: To transmit electrical signals over long distances in the body
To absorb oxygen from the air
CS.explanation: The axon allows nerve cells to send signals quickly from the brain or spinal cord to distant parts of the body.
CS.hint: Think about what nerve cells need to do to communicate with distant body parts.

CS.q: Plant cells have a cell wall made of cellulose. What is the main advantage of this structure?
options: It provides rigidity and structural support to the plant
It controls what enters and leaves the cell
CS.explanation: The cell wall is rigid and provides mechanical support, allowing plants to stand upright and maintain their shape.
CS.hint: Why can plants stand up without a skeleton?

CS.q: A red blood cell loses its nucleus when it matures. How does this help the cell function?
options: It allows more space to carry oxygen
It makes the cell move faster
It prevents the cell from dividing
CS.explanation: Without a nucleus, the red blood cell has more cytoplasm space to carry haemoglobin and oxygen molecules throughout the body.
CS.hint: What is the main job of a red blood cell?

CS.q: The stomach is an organ made of several different tissues. Which tissues are found in the stomach wall?
options: Muscle tissue, epithelial tissue, and connective tissue
CS.explanation: The stomach wall contains muscle tissue (for churning), epithelial tissue (lining the stomach), and connective tissue (holding layers together).
CS.hint: An organ needs to move, protect, and support itself.

CS.q: Which of the following is the correct order of organisation from smallest to largest?
CS.explanation: Cells are the smallest living units. Similar cells form tissues, tissues form organs, and organs work together in organ systems.
CS.hint: Start with the smallest unit of life and build up.

CS.q: A student observes a cheek cell under a microscope at ×400 magnification. The nucleus appears 0.2 mm in diameter. What is the actual diameter of the nucleus?
CS.explanation: Real size = image size ÷ magnification = 0.2 ÷ 400 = 0.0005 mm.
CS.hint: Remember: real size = image size divided by magnification.

CS.q: Which organelle is responsible for releasing energy in both plant and animal cells through aerobic respiration?
CS.explanation: Mitochondria are the powerhouses of the cell, breaking down glucose to release energy in the form of ATP during aerobic respiration.
CS.hint: Think about which organelle produces the energy currency of the cell.

CS.q: A plant cell is placed in a hypertonic solution (high salt concentration). What will happen to the cell?
options: The cell will swell and burst
The cytoplasm will shrink away from the cell wall
CS.explanation: In a hypertonic solution, water leaves the cell by osmosis, causing the cytoplasm to shrink away from the cell wall. This is called plasmolysis.
CS.hint: Hypertonic means the solution has more solutes than the cell. Which direction will water move?

CS.q: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between cells, tissues, and organs in a multicellular organism?
options: Organs are made of cells, tissues are made of organs
Cells are made of tissues, tissues are made of organs
Tissues are made of cells, organs are made of tissues
Organs are made of tissues, tissues are made of organs
CS.explanation: The correct hierarchy is: cells form tissues, tissues combine to form organs. For example, muscle cells form muscle tissue, which is part of the heart organ.
CS.hint: Think of the smallest unit (cells) and how they build up into larger structures.

CS.q: A ciliated epithelial tissue lines the trachea. What is the main function of this tissue?
options: To move mucus and trapped particles out of the lungs
CS.explanation: Ciliated epithelium in the trachea beats in coordinated waves to move mucus containing trapped dust and pathogens upward, protecting the lungs.
CS.hint: Think about what needs to be removed from the respiratory system and how cilia move.

CS.q: Which statement best explains why specialised cells have many mitochondria?
options: They need more mitochondria to store food
They perform functions that require large amounts of energy
Mitochondria protect the cell from damage
Multiple mitochondria help cells divide faster
CS.explanation: Cells with high energy demands, like muscle cells and nerve cells, contain many mitochondria to produce sufficient ATP through aerobic respiration.
CS.hint: Think about which body cells work the hardest and need the most energy.

MS.q: In a sugar solution, what is the solvent?
MS.explanation: Water is the liquid that dissolves the sugar — the solvent.
MS.hint: The solvent is the liquid part of a solution.

MS.q: Which of these is a mixture?
MS.explanation: Salt water contains salt and water mixed together — it is a mixture.
MS.hint: A mixture has two or more substances that are not chemically joined.

MS.q: What is the name of the solid left behind when sea water is evaporated?
MS.explanation: When sea water evaporates, salt crystallises and is left as a solid residue.
MS.hint: What is the main dissolved substance in sea water?

MS.q: Which method would you use to separate dye colours in an ink sample?
MS.explanation: Chromatography separates different coloured dyes by moving them at different speeds through paper.
MS.hint: This method is used to separate colours and pigments.

MS.q: Which statement correctly describes a pure substance?
options: It contains only one type of particle
MS.explanation: A pure substance contains only one type of particle or molecule throughout.
MS.hint: A pure substance has a fixed composition.

MS.q: A student heats water until it boils and collects the steam. What will they collect?
MS.explanation: Steam is pure water vapour — any dissolved salts remain behind because they do not evaporate with the water.
MS.hint: Only the liquid water turns into a gas and leaves as steam.

MS.q: In a sugar solution, what is the solvent?
MS.explanation: The solvent is the liquid that dissolves the solute. Water dissolves the sugar.
MS.hint: Which part is the liquid?

MS.q: Which of the following is a mixture?
MS.explanation: Salt water contains two substances (salt and water) mixed together, making it a mixture.
MS.hint: A mixture has more than one substance that can be separated.

MS.q: What does evaporation leave behind when salt water is heated?
MS.explanation: Salt does not evaporate with water, so it stays in the container as crystals.
MS.hint: Which part has a higher boiling point?

MS.q: Which separation method uses paper to separate insoluble solids from liquids?
MS.explanation: Filtration uses filter paper with tiny holes. Liquids pass through but solid particles are trapped.
MS.hint: The word contains 'filter'.

MS.q: Which method separates different coloured dyes in ink?
MS.explanation: Chromatography separates substances by how they move at different rates up absorbent paper.
MS.hint: This method creates a colourful pattern on paper.

MS.q: What is a saturated solution?
options: A solution where no more solute can dissolve
A solution made with hot water
MS.explanation: A saturated solution contains the maximum amount of dissolved solute at that temperature.
MS.hint: Think about when you cannot dissolve any more sugar in your tea.

MS.q: Why is distillation used to separate salt from seawater but not to separate sand from seawater?
options: Sand dissolves in water but salt does not
Salt has a higher boiling point than water
Sand does not dissolve in water, so it can be filtered out first
Distillation only works with dissolved solids
MS.explanation: Sand is insoluble and settles, so it can be removed by filtration before distillation. Distillation then separates the dissolved salt from water.
MS.hint: Think about what each substance does in water - does it dissolve or not?

MS.q: Which statement about paper chromatography is correct?
options: The solvent moves down the paper by gravity
Dyes with smaller molecules travel further up the paper
The baseline must be drawn in pencil, not pen
All dyes in a mixture travel at the same speed
MS.explanation: The baseline must be in pencil because pen ink would dissolve in the solvent and interfere with the results.
MS.hint: What do you draw the baseline with? Why can't you use certain materials?

MS.q: A substance dissolves completely in water at room temperature. After cooling the solution in a freezer, crystals form at the bottom. What is this process called?
MS.explanation: Crystallisation is when a dissolved solid forms solid crystals as the solution cools or evaporates.
MS.hint: What happens when a solution becomes too concentrated for the dissolved solid to stay dissolved?

MS.q: In fractional distillation, liquids are separated in order of their boiling points. Which liquid would come out of the fractionating column first?
options: The liquid with the highest boiling point
The liquid with the lowest boiling point
The liquid that is least dense
The liquid that is most soluble in water
MS.explanation: The liquid with the lowest boiling point evaporates first and rises to the coolest part of the column, so it is collected first.
MS.hint: Which liquid needs the least heat to turn into a gas?

MS.q: Which separation technique would be most suitable for separating a mixture of two immiscible liquids with similar boiling points?
MS.explanation: A separating funnel is used to separate immiscible liquids (liquids that do not mix) regardless of their boiling points.
MS.hint: Immiscible means the liquids don't mix - think of oil and water.

MS.q: Which separation technique would you use to separate salt from a saltwater solution?
MS.explanation: Evaporation heats the solution to remove the water, leaving behind solid salt crystals. Filtration cannot separate dissolved substances.
MS.hint: The salt is dissolved in water. What happens if you heat the solution?

MS.q: In paper chromatography, a dye travels 6 cm while the solvent travels 10 cm. What is the Rf value?
MS.explanation: Rf = distance travelled by substance ÷ distance travelled by solvent = 6 ÷ 10 = 0.6
MS.hint: Rf value = divide the distances

MS.q: A substance has a melting point range of 45°C to 52°C. What does this suggest?
options: It is a gas at room temperature
MS.explanation: A wide melting point range indicates the substance is impure. Pure substances have a sharp, fixed melting point.
MS.hint: Compare to a pure substance which melts at one exact temperature.

MS.q: Which method would separate sand from water in a muddy sand-water mixture?
MS.explanation: Filtration separates insoluble solids (sand) from liquids (water) using a filter paper.
MS.hint: Sand does not dissolve in water - it is insoluble.

MS.q: Pure water boils at exactly 100°C. A sample of water boils between 99°C and 101°C. What conclusion can you draw?
options: The water is being heated too quickly
MS.explanation: A boiling point range indicates the presence of dissolved impurities. Pure water has a fixed boiling point of exactly 100°C.
MS.hint: Compare the boiling point range to what happens with pure water.

EE.q: What is the SI unit of electrical current?
EE.explanation: The SI unit of electrical current is the Amp (ampere).
EE.hint: An ammeter measures this unit.

EE.q: Which of the following is a conductor of electricity?
EE.explanation: Aluminum is a metal and conducts electricity well. Rubber, glass and wood are insulators.
EE.hint: Metals are usually good conductors.

EE.q: What does a voltmeter measure?
EE.explanation: A voltmeter measures the potential difference (voltage) across a component in volts.
EE.hint: Volt — it's in the name!

EE.q: In a parallel circuit, what happens to the total current when you add more branches?
EE.explanation: When you add more branches in a parallel circuit, more paths are available for current to flow, so the total current from the power source increases.
EE.hint: More branches = more paths for current to take.

EE.q: Which symbol represents a cell (battery) in an electrical circuit diagram?
options: A long line and a short line
EE.explanation: A cell is represented by one long line and one short line. A battery is two or more cells.
EE.hint: The long line is the positive terminal.

EE.q: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
EE.explanation: Electrical resistance is measured in ohms (Ω).
EE.hint: It sounds like the Greek letter omega.

EE.q: What is the SI unit of electrical current?
EE.explanation: The SI unit of current is the amp (ampere), named after André-Marie Ampère.
EE.hint: Think about the symbol 'A' used on meters.

EE.q: Which of the following is a conductor?
EE.explanation: Aluminium is a metal that allows electricity to flow through it easily — it is a conductor.
EE.hint: Conductors are usually shiny metals.

EE.q: What is the symbol for voltage in equations?
EE.explanation: V is the symbol for voltage, measured in volts (V).
EE.hint: V stands for 'volt'.

EE.q: Which component stores and releases electrical energy?
EE.explanation: A battery stores chemical energy and converts it into electrical energy to power a circuit.
EE.hint: Look for the portable source of power.

EE.q: What does the law of conservation of energy state?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only changed from one form to another
Energy only exists in moving objects
EE.explanation: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another (e.g., chemical to electrical to light).
EE.hint: Think about whether energy disappears or just changes.

EE.q: What is the SI unit of electrical current?
EE.explanation: The ampere (A) is the SI unit used to measure electrical current.
EE.hint: Think of the device that measures current — an ammeter.

EE.q: Two identical bulbs are connected in series to a battery. If one bulb fails and breaks the circuit, what happens to the other bulb?
EE.explanation: In a series circuit, if one component breaks, the circuit is broken and current cannot flow anywhere. Both bulbs go off.
EE.hint: In series, there is only one path for current to follow.

EE.q: A parallel circuit has two branches, each with a resistor. How does the total resistance compare to a single resistor?
options: It is higher than either single resistor
It is the same as one resistor
It is lower than either single resistor
EE.explanation: In parallel circuits, adding more branches provides more paths for current, reducing total resistance below any single resistor value.
EE.hint: More paths = easier for current to flow.

EE.q: A mobile phone battery stores 20,000 joules of energy. If the phone uses 10 joules per second, how long will the battery last?
EE.explanation: Time = Energy ÷ Power. Time = 20,000 J ÷ 10 J/s = 2,000 seconds (about 33 minutes).
EE.hint: Power is joules per second. Divide energy by power.

EE.q: Which statement about renewable and non-renewable energy sources is correct?
options: Renewable sources can run out eventually
Solar energy is renewed naturally by the Sun
EE.explanation: Solar energy is renewable because the Sun continuously produces energy and will do so for billions of years. The other statements are false.
EE.hint: 'Renewable' means it is naturally replaced or will last very long.

EE.q: A solar panel on a roof transfers light energy to electrical energy. What happens to some of the light energy that is not converted to electricity?
options: It is stored in the panel
It is reflected back to the Sun
EE.explanation: Solar panels are not 100% efficient. Some light energy is absorbed by the panel material and released as heat, warming the panel.
EE.hint: No energy conversion is 100% efficient. Where does 'wasted' energy go?

EE.q: A power station generates 1,000,000 joules of energy. If it took 1 minute to generate this energy, what was the power output?
EE.explanation: Power = Energy ÷ Time = 1,000,000 J ÷ 60 s = 16,667 W (approximately).
EE.hint: Power is measured in watts. 1 watt = 1 joule per second.

EE.q: A student builds a parallel circuit with two identical resistors. How does the total resistance compare to a series circuit with the same two resistors?
options: Both circuits have the same resistance
It depends on the battery voltage
EE.explanation: In parallel circuits, current has multiple paths, so total resistance is lower than in series. For identical resistors, parallel resistance is half the series resistance.
EE.hint: More paths for current = easier flow = lower resistance.

EE.q: A kettle has a power rating of 2000 W. Which statement about energy transfer is correct?
options: It transfers 2000 W of heat energy
It stores 2000 J in the water
EE.explanation: Power is measured in watts (W), where 1 W = 1 J/s. So 2000 W means 2000 joules of energy transferred every second.
EE.hint: Power = energy ÷ time. What is power measured in joules per?

EE.q: In a household circuit, a fuse or circuit breaker is designed to:
options: Increase the current flowing through appliances
Prevent too much current from causing a fire
EE.explanation: Fuses and circuit breakers protect circuits by breaking the connection if current becomes dangerously high, preventing overheating and potential fires.
EE.hint: What safety feature stops current if something goes wrong?

EE.q: Which renewable energy source is most reliable because it works day and night?
EE.explanation: Geothermal power uses heat from inside the Earth, which is constant day and night. Solar only works during daylight and wind varies throughout the day.
EE.hint: Which energy source does not depend on weather or time of day?

EI.q: Which of the following is a consumer in a food chain?
EI.explanation: A consumer is an organism that eats other organisms for food. The rabbit eats grass, making it a consumer. Plants are producers, and soil is neither.
EI.hint: A consumer has to eat something else to survive.

EI.q: What do decomposers do in an ecosystem?
options: Break down dead organisms and return nutrients to soil
Make their own food using sunlight
EI.explanation: Decomposers like bacteria and fungi break down dead matter and recycle nutrients back into the ecosystem.
EI.hint: Think about what happens to dead leaves on the forest floor.

EI.q: In a food chain, which organism has the least energy?
EI.explanation: Energy decreases as it moves up the food chain. The secondary consumer is highest in the chain shown, so it has the least energy.
EI.hint: Energy is lost at each stage of the food chain.

EI.q: Which of the following shows a correct food chain?
EI.explanation: A food chain must start with a producer (grass) and show the correct feeding direction: grass is eaten by rabbit, and rabbit is eaten by fox.
EI.hint: Remember: arrows point FROM food TO the thing eating it.

EI.q: What is a food web?
options: A simple linear chain of organisms
Many interconnected food chains showing different feeding relationships
EI.explanation: A food web is more complex than a single food chain—it shows multiple food chains linked together, representing the real feeding relationships in an ecosystem.
EI.hint: A web has many connections, while a chain is just one line.

EI.q: Which of the following is a primary consumer in a food chain?
EI.explanation: A primary consumer eats plants (producers). The zebra is a herbivore that eats grass.
EI.hint: Primary consumers eat plants, not other animals.

EI.q: In the food chain: lettuce → caterpillar → bird → cat, what is the cat?
EI.explanation: The cat is at the top of this chain, eating the bird (secondary consumer), making it a tertiary consumer.
EI.hint: Count how many steps up from the producer the cat is.

EI.q: What term describes when one animal hunts and eats another animal?
EI.explanation: Predation is the relationship where a predator hunts and eats prey.
EI.hint: Think of a word that sounds like 'predator'.

EI.q: Which organism breaks down dead material and returns nutrients to the soil?
EI.explanation: Decomposers like bacteria and fungi break down dead organisms and recycle nutrients.
EI.hint: These organisms 'decompose' (break down) dead things.

EI.q: A ladybird eats aphids (small insects). What type of relationship is this?
EI.explanation: Predation is when one organism (ladybird) hunts and eats another (aphids).
EI.hint: One animal is hunting the other for food.

EI.q: Which of these is an example of competition in an ecosystem?
options: Two rabbits eating grass from the same field
A fungus breaking down a log
EI.explanation: Competition occurs when two organisms need the same resource. Two rabbits competing for the same grass is competition.
EI.hint: Competition is when organisms struggle for the same thing they need.

EI.q: Which of the following is an example of a predator?
EI.explanation: A predator is an animal that hunts and eats other animals. A lion hunts other animals for food.
EI.hint: Think of an animal that hunts other animals for food.

EI.q: Which of the following is an example of a parasite-host relationship?
options: A tapeworm living in a human intestine
A remora fish attaching to a shark
EI.explanation: A parasite lives on or in a host and harms it. A tapeworm feeds on nutrients inside the human body, benefiting itself while harming the host.
EI.hint: A parasite benefits itself but harms its partner.

EI.q: A clownfish lives safely among sea anemone tentacles that would sting other fish. The anemone gets leftover food scraps from the clownfish. This relationship is called:
EI.explanation: Mutualism is when both organisms benefit. The clownfish gains protection and the anemone gets food. Both organisms benefit from living together.
EI.hint: Both animals are gaining something from this partnership.

EI.q: Two species of bird live in the same woodland and eat the same insects. Over time, one species begins to hunt only at night while the other hunts during the day. This is an example of:
EI.explanation: The two species are reducing competition by occupying different niches (time periods). Each species now feeds in a different way, allowing both to survive.
EI.hint: What has changed to reduce competition between them?

EI.q: Which statement best describes competition in an ecosystem?
options: Two species hunting each other for food
Two species fighting over the same resources like food or space
One species living inside another species
Two species helping each other to survive
EI.explanation: Competition occurs when organisms fight over limited resources such as food, water, light, or space. This can happen between species (interspecific) or within a species (intraspecific).
EI.hint: Competition is about fighting over what?

EI.q: In a forest, a pine tree and a fungus form a mycorrhizal relationship where the fungus grows around the tree's roots. The fungus gets sugars from the tree, and the tree gains better water and mineral absorption. Which organisms benefit from this interaction?
options: Both the tree and the fungus
EI.explanation: Both organisms gain advantages from this relationship. The fungus obtains sugars for energy, while the tree gains improved access to water and minerals. This is mutualism.
EI.hint: Identify what each organism gets from the relationship.

EI.q: Which of the following is an example of a predator-prey relationship?
options: A remora fish and a shark
EI.explanation: A predator hunts and eats prey. Lions hunt zebras. Bees and flowers have a mutualistic relationship (both benefit). Remora fish benefit from the shark but the shark is unaffected (commensalism). Clownfish and anemones both benefit (mutualism).
EI.hint: A predator kills and eats another animal. Which pair shows one hunting the other?

EI.q: What happens to the grass population if all the rabbits in a meadow die from disease?
EI.explanation: Rabbits eat grass (they are herbivores). Without rabbits to graze, more grass survives and grows, so the population increases.
EI.hint: Rabbits eat grass. What happens to food when consumers are removed?

EI.q: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria live in the roots of pea plants. The bacteria get sugars from the plant, and the plant gets nitrogen compounds. This relationship is called…
EI.explanation: Mutualism is when both organisms benefit. The bacteria receive sugars (energy) and the plant receives nitrogen (nutrients). Both gain an advantage.
EI.hint: Both organisms benefit from this relationship. What do we call it when both win?

EI.q: A parasite differs from a predator because a parasite…
options: Lives on or inside its host and feeds without killing it
EI.explanation: Parasites live on or inside a host organism and feed on it over time without immediately killing it (this would harm their food source). Predators hunt, kill and eat their prey immediately.
EI.hint: A parasite needs its host to stay alive for a long time. Why would a predator kill immediately?

EI.q: Which statement best explains why an ecosystem needs producers?
options: Producers eat all the waste from consumers
Producers capture energy from the sun and make it available to all other organisms
Producers are the only organisms that need energy
Producers prevent predators from eating herbivores
EI.explanation: Producers (plants) convert sunlight into chemical energy through photosynthesis. This energy enters the ecosystem and flows through all food chains. Without producers, there would be no energy source for any other organism.
EI.hint: Think about where all the energy in an ecosystem comes from.

EI.q: In a food chain, energy is lost at each trophic level mainly through which process?
EI.explanation: Organisms use energy for respiration (movement, thinking, maintaining body heat), which releases energy as heat that cannot be recaptured by the next trophic level.
EI.hint: Think about what happens to energy when an organism moves or keeps warm.

EI.q: Which statement best explains why a parasitic relationship (like fleas on a dog) is different from a predator-prey relationship?
options: Parasites feed on their host over a long period without killing it quickly
Parasites always live outside the body
EI.explanation: Parasites depend on their host's survival for their own survival, so they do not kill the host quickly. Predators kill and eat their prey completely.
EI.hint: Does a flea kill a dog quickly or slowly?

EI.q: In a stable ecosystem, what does the cycling of nutrients between biotic and abiotic components primarily depend on?
options: Decomposers breaking down dead material and returning elements to the soil
EI.explanation: Decomposers are essential for nutrient cycling because they break down dead organisms and waste, returning nutrients (like nitrogen and carbon) to the soil and atmosphere so producers can use them again.
EI.hint: What organism breaks down dead leaves and returns nutrients to soil?

EI.q: Why do decomposers form the base of nutrient cycling rather than food chains?
options: They break down all organisms and return nutrients to the environment, not just transferring energy
EI.explanation: While food chains show energy transfer, decomposers are crucial for nutrient cycling because they break down all dead matter and release chemical elements (like nitrogen, phosphorus) back into soil and water, making them available for producers again.
EI.hint: What is the difference between energy transfer and nutrient return?

EI.q: A food web shows multiple interconnected food chains in an ecosystem. Why is a food web more useful than a single food chain for understanding real ecosystems?
options: Real organisms eat multiple food sources, and food webs show how removing one species affects many others
Food chains are too easy to draw
Food webs show the same information but faster
EI.explanation: Food webs realistically show that most organisms have multiple food sources and are food sources for multiple predators. This helps predict cascading effects when one species is removed or population changes.
EI.hint: Does a rabbit only eat one type of plant?

EI.q: In a food chain: grass → rabbit → fox → wolf, which organism has the least available energy?
EI.explanation: The wolf is at the highest trophic level. Energy decreases by approximately 90% at each step up the food chain, so top predators have the least energy available.
EI.hint: Think about which organism is furthest from the original energy source (the Sun).

EI.q: A population of deer increases dramatically after all the wolves in an area are removed. What is the most likely consequence for the vegetation?
options: Vegetation increases because deer are removed
Vegetation decreases due to overgrazing by the increased deer population
Wolves eat vegetation, so removing them helps plants grow
EI.explanation: Without the wolf predator controlling deer numbers, the deer population grows unchecked and overgrazes the vegetation, reducing plant biomass.
EI.hint: Think about what happens to a population when its main predator is removed.

EI.q: Why do decomposers occupy a unique position in food webs rather than being part of a linear food chain?
options: They break down dead organic matter from all trophic levels and return nutrients to the soil, feeding on material from multiple levels
They are not important to ecosystems
EI.explanation: Decomposers consume dead organisms and waste from all trophic levels, not just one level above them, so they interact with the entire food web structure.
EI.hint: Where does a decomposer get its food from compared to a consumer?

PM.q: Which state of matter has particles that are tightly packed and vibrate in fixed positions?
PM.explanation: Solid particles are tightly packed together and can only vibrate in fixed positions, giving solids a definite shape and volume.
PM.hint: Think of something hard like a rock or metal.

PM.q: Liquid → gas is called…
PM.explanation: Liquid to gas is evaporating, like water turning into steam when heated.
PM.hint: What happens to puddles on a sunny day?

PM.q: Why do solids have a definite shape?
options: Particles are in fixed positions and held by strong forces
PM.explanation: Solids have a definite shape because their particles are held in fixed positions by strong attractive forces between them.
PM.hint: What keeps solid particles from moving around?

PM.q: Which statement about gas particles is true?
options: They move slowly in all directions
They are far apart and move very quickly
They are arranged in a fixed pattern
PM.explanation: Gas particles are far apart, move very quickly in all directions, and have no fixed arrangement.
PM.hint: What fills a balloon or a room?

PM.q: What is the correct order of state changes from solid to gas?
PM.explanation: A solid first melts to become a liquid, then the liquid evaporates to become a gas.
PM.hint: Think about what happens to ice when it warms up.

PM.q: In which state of matter are the particles closest together?
options: All states have particles equally spaced
PM.explanation: Solid particles are closest together because they are tightly packed in fixed positions.
PM.hint: Which state takes up the least amount of space?

PM.q: Which state of matter has particles that are tightly packed and cannot move around?
PM.explanation: Solid particles are tightly packed in fixed positions and can only vibrate.
PM.hint: Think about something you can hold in your hand that keeps its shape.

PM.q: Liquid → gas is called…
PM.explanation: Liquid to gas is evaporating, like water turning into steam.
PM.hint: What happens to puddles on a sunny day?

PM.q: Which of the following best describes gas particles?
options: Tightly packed and fixed in position
PM.explanation: Gas particles are far apart and move randomly in all directions at high speeds.
PM.hint: Think about air or smoke spreading throughout a room.

PM.q: Gas → solid is called…
PM.explanation: Gas to solid is deposition, the opposite of sublimation.
PM.hint: This is like frost forming on windows in winter.

PM.q: Which state of matter has a fixed shape and a fixed volume?
PM.explanation: Only solids have both a fixed shape and fixed volume because their particles are in fixed positions.
PM.hint: Which one doesn't change shape when you move it?

PM.q: What is the name of the process when liquid → solid?
PM.explanation: Liquid to solid is freezing, like water turning into ice.
PM.hint: This is the opposite of melting.

PM.q: Why do gases have no fixed shape or volume?
options: Gas particles move randomly in all directions with large spaces between them
PM.explanation: Gas particles move randomly with lots of space between them, so they spread out to fill any container and have no fixed shape or volume.
PM.hint: Think about how gas particles move and how far apart they are.

PM.q: A block of ice is left in a warm room. Solid water particles vibrate more vigorously. What does this lead to?
options: The ice melts because forces between particles are overcome
The ice sublimates directly to gas
The particles move apart but remain solid
PM.explanation: As particles vibrate more due to heating, they eventually vibrate fast enough to overcome the forces holding them in fixed positions, causing melting.
PM.hint: What happens to forces between particles when they vibrate faster?

PM.q: Why does a puddle of water eventually disappear on a sunny day without reaching 100°C?
options: The water is absorbed into the ground
Evaporation occurs at the surface where some particles have enough energy to escape
Wind blows all the water away
PM.explanation: Evaporation happens at any temperature when particles at the surface have enough kinetic energy to overcome forces and escape into the air.
PM.hint: Not all particles need the same energy to leave the liquid surface.

PM.q: Which process involves particles gaining enough energy to overcome all forces between them and escape into the air?
PM.explanation: Vaporisation (boiling or evaporation) is when particles gain enough energy to completely overcome forces and become a gas.
PM.hint: Which change of state takes a liquid to a gas?

PM.q: During freezing, what happens to the particles as a liquid cools down?
options: Particles vibrate faster and move closer together
Particles vibrate slower and arrange into a fixed pattern
PM.explanation: As temperature drops during freezing, particles vibrate less. They slow down enough that forces between them hold them in fixed, orderly positions forming a solid.
PM.hint: What happens to particle vibration as temperature decreases?

PM.q: A container of gas is cooled while keeping the volume constant. What happens to the pressure?
options: The gas changes state to liquid
PM.explanation: When cooled, gas particles move slower and hit the container walls less often and with less force, so the pressure decreases.
PM.hint: How does the motion of particles change when temperature drops?

PM.q: A liquid is cooled and turns into a solid. What happens to the movement of particles during this change?
options: Particles move faster and spread further apart
Particles move more slowly and vibrate in fixed positions
Particles move in the same way but change colour
PM.explanation: During freezing, particles lose energy, move more slowly, and become arranged in fixed positions characteristic of a solid state.
PM.hint: What happens to particle energy when a substance cools down?

PM.q: A block of ice is left in a warm room. During melting, the ice remains at 0°C until it is completely melted. What does this tell us about the energy being supplied?
options: No energy is being supplied to the ice
The energy is making particles move faster to increase temperature
The energy is used to break the fixed structure, not increase particle speed
The energy is being lost to the surroundings
PM.explanation: During a change of state, the supplied energy breaks the forces holding particles in their fixed positions rather than increasing their kinetic energy, so temperature remains constant.
PM.hint: What must happen to the forces between particles during melting?

PM.q: Which of the following best explains why a solid has a fixed shape?
options: Particles in a solid don't move at all
Particles are tightly packed and vibrate in fixed positions around strong attractive forces
Particles are very large and cannot move
The solid is held in place by gravity
PM.explanation: In a solid, particles vibrate around fixed positions due to strong forces between them, giving the solid its rigid shape.
PM.hint: Are particles in a solid completely still or do they have some movement?

PM.q: When a gas is compressed into a smaller volume at constant temperature, what happens to the pressure?
options: Pressure decreases because particles have less space
Pressure increases because particles collide with walls more frequently
Pressure stays the same because temperature hasn't changed
PM.explanation: Compressing a gas reduces the volume, so particles hit the walls in a smaller space more frequently, increasing pressure even though temperature and particle speed remain constant.
PM.hint: How does reducing space affect how often particles hit the walls?

RV.q: Which of the following is made in the ovaries?
RV.explanation: Egg cells are produced in the ovaries in females.
RV.hint: The female reproductive organ that produces eggs.

RV.q: What is variation?
options: Differences between organisms of the same species
The joining of sperm and egg
RV.explanation: Variation refers to the differences in characteristics between individual organisms of the same species.
RV.hint: Think about why your classmates look different from each other.

RV.q: In plants, which structure contains the male gamete?
RV.explanation: Pollen contains the male gamete (sperm cell) in plants.
RV.hint: The powder-like substance that causes hay fever.

RV.q: Which of these is an example of inherited variation?
RV.explanation: Eye colour is inherited from your parents through genes, making it inherited variation.
RV.hint: This characteristic comes from your parents' genes.

RV.q: What happens during pollination in flowers?
options: Pollen is transferred from the anther to the stigma
RV.explanation: Pollination is the transfer of pollen from the anther to the stigma of a flower.
RV.hint: It involves the transfer of pollen between flower parts.

RV.q: Which tube carries the egg from the ovary to the uterus?
RV.explanation: The oviduct (fallopian tube) carries the egg from the ovary to the uterus.
RV.hint: It is also called the fallopian tube.

RV.q: Which of the following is a female reproductive organ?
RV.explanation: The ovary is a female reproductive organ that produces eggs. The other options are all male reproductive structures.
RV.hint: Think about where eggs are made in females.

RV.q: What is the tube called that carries the egg from the ovary to the uterus?
RV.explanation: The oviduct (also called fallopian tube) carries the egg from the ovary to the uterus where fertilisation usually occurs.
RV.hint: It starts with 'O' and carries the egg.

RV.q: What is the name of the cell that is produced when a sperm and egg join together?
RV.explanation: A zygote is the cell formed when a sperm nucleus fuses with an egg nucleus during fertilisation.
RV.hint: It's what forms immediately after fertilisation.

RV.q: Which process produces variation in offspring?
RV.explanation: Sexual reproduction produces variation because genetic material from two different parents combines. Asexual reproduction produces identical offspring.
RV.hint: It involves two parents contributing genes.

RV.q: What is the role of the cervix in the female reproductive system?
options: To connect the uterus to the vagina
RV.explanation: The cervix is the narrow opening at the bottom of the uterus that connects it to the vagina. It allows sperm to enter and babies to pass through during birth.
RV.hint: It's a passage or opening between two organs.

RV.q: Which structure in sperm helps it to swim towards the egg?
RV.explanation: The tail (flagellum) of a sperm cell moves side-to-side to propel the sperm forward through the female reproductive tract towards the egg.
RV.hint: It's the long structure at the back of the sperm.

RV.q: Which of the following is an example of a variation caused only by genes?
RV.explanation: Blood type is inherited and cannot be changed by the environment. Skin tone can be affected by sun exposure, muscle size by exercise, and hair length by cutting.
RV.hint: Which trait cannot be changed by what you do or where you live?

RV.q: What is the main function of the placenta during pregnancy?
options: To exchange nutrients and oxygen between mother and fetus
To protect the baby from infection
To control the baby's heart rate
RV.explanation: The placenta allows the exchange of nutrients, oxygen, and waste between the mother's blood and the fetus's blood, without them mixing directly.
RV.hint: Think about what the baby needs to survive and grow in the womb.

RV.q: Which statement about asexual reproduction is correct?
options: Offspring are genetically identical to the parent
It involves the fusion of gametes
RV.explanation: Asexual reproduction uses only one parent and produces offspring that are clones - genetically identical. Sexual reproduction involves two parents and creates variation.
RV.hint: Does asexual reproduction need sperm and egg to mix?

RV.q: During meiosis, what happens to the number of chromosomes?
RV.explanation: Meiosis produces gametes (sex cells) with half the number of chromosomes as the parent cell. This is important so that when sperm and egg fuse, the baby has the normal number.
RV.hint: Sex cells need fewer chromosomes than body cells - why?

RV.q: Which of these is a characteristic that shows continuous variation?
options: Presence or absence of a widow's peak
RV.explanation: Height shows continuous variation - it can be any value across a range. Freckles, earlobes, and widow's peaks show discontinuous variation - you either have them or don't.
RV.hint: Which one can be measured as many different values, not just yes or no?

RV.q: What is the role of the umbilical cord?
options: To attach the baby to the amniotic sac
To protect the baby from infections
To carry nutrients and oxygen from the placenta to the baby
To produce hormones that control pregnancy
RV.explanation: The umbilical cord is a tube that connects the baby to the placenta. It carries oxygen and nutrients to the baby and removes waste products.
RV.hint: Think of it as a tube connecting the baby to its food and oxygen supply.

RV.q: Which of the following is an example of a variation caused ONLY by genes?
RV.explanation: Fingerprints are unique to each person and determined entirely by genes. They do not change with environment or lifestyle.
RV.hint: Which feature stays exactly the same throughout your life and cannot be changed by anything you do?

RV.q: During sexual reproduction, what is the main advantage of inheriting genetic material from both parents?
options: It creates genetic variation in the population
The offspring is guaranteed to be healthy
It requires less energy than asexual reproduction
RV.explanation: Genetic material from two parents combines in different ways, creating variation. This variation helps populations adapt to changing environments.
RV.hint: How does having genes from two different sources create something new?

RV.q: Which statement correctly describes the role of the umbilical cord?
options: It produces hormones to control the baby's growth
It carries blood containing nutrients and oxygen from mother to baby
It protects the baby from infections
It removes only waste products from the baby
RV.explanation: The umbilical cord contains blood vessels that transport oxygen, nutrients, and waste between the mother and developing baby.
RV.hint: Think of it like a pipeline connecting the baby to the mother's blood supply.

RV.q: Why is the placenta an important organ during pregnancy?
options: It is where the baby's heart develops
It allows nutrient and oxygen exchange between mother and baby
It controls how fast the baby grows
RV.explanation: The placenta is embedded in the uterus wall and allows oxygen and nutrients to pass from mother's blood to baby's blood, and waste products to pass back.
RV.hint: What structure needs to pass materials between two separate blood systems?

RV.q: In humans, eye colour is controlled by multiple genes. Why does this produce a range of eye colours rather than just two distinct types?
options: Because eye colour is only determined by the environment
Because multiple genes contribute additive effects, creating continuous variation
Because eye colour cannot be inherited
Because mutations always occur in eye colour genes
RV.explanation: When multiple genes control a trait, their combined effects produce a spectrum of phenotypes rather than distinct categories. This is polygenic inheritance.
RV.hint: Think about how several genes working together can add up to create a range.

RV.q: A plant produces seeds through sexual reproduction. Which statement best explains why the seeds are genetically different from each other and from the parent plant?
options: Meiosis produces gametes with random combinations of chromosomes and genes
The environment always causes genetic mutations
Seeds cannot inherit genes from parents
RV.explanation: Meiosis shuffles chromosomes randomly, and fusion of two different gametes creates genetic variation in offspring, making them different from each other and parents.
RV.hint: What process in sexual reproduction mixes up genes and chromosomes?

RV.q: A scientist crosses two heterozygous tall pea plants (Tt) that produce offspring in a 3:1 ratio (tall:short). This demonstrates…
options: Dominance, as the T allele masks the t allele in heterozygotes
RV.explanation: The 3:1 ratio is the classic Mendelian ratio showing that T (tall) is dominant over t (short). Heterozygotes show the dominant phenotype.
RV.hint: Remember: 3 tall to 1 short is the signature of dominant/recessive alleles.

RV.q: A gardener observes that offspring from a self-pollinating flower show less variation than expected. The most likely explanation is…
options: Meiosis produces greater variation in self-pollination
The flower is a pure-breeding line with homozygous alleles, producing little genetic variation
Self-pollination creates new mutations in every offspring
RV.explanation: If a plant is homozygous at most loci, self-pollination will continue to produce homozygous offspring with minimal genetic variation.
RV.hint: What if the parent flower has mostly identical gene pairs (homozygous)?

RV.q: Human skin colour is controlled by multiple genes. A child born to parents with different skin colours often has an intermediate shade. This is best explained by…
options: Polygenic inheritance, where alleles combine additively
Environmental factors alone determining skin colour
The child always inheriting skin colour from one parent only
RV.explanation: Multiple genes each contribute small effects to skin colour, producing a range. Alleles from both parents combine additively, resulting in intermediate phenotypes.
RV.hint: Many genes + additive effects = what type of variation?

WS.q: What is the name of a single complete back-and-forth movement of a vibrating object?
WS.explanation: An oscillation is one complete vibration, going back and forth once.
WS.hint: Think of a pendulum swinging once from one side to the other and back.

WS.q: Which of these is an example of a longitudinal wave?
WS.explanation: Sound waves are longitudinal waves because the particles vibrate in the same direction as the wave travels.
WS.hint: In which wave do particles move in the same direction as the wave?

WS.q: What does the wavelength of a wave measure?
options: The time taken for one wave
The distance from one wave crest to the next crest
WS.explanation: Wavelength is the distance between two identical points on a wave, such as from one crest to the next crest.
WS.hint: Look at the distance between the highest points of a wave.

WS.q: Which of the following correctly describes the amplitude of a wave?
options: The number of waves per second
The distance the wave travels in one second
The maximum distance a particle moves from its resting position
The distance from one crest to the next trough
WS.explanation: Amplitude is how far a particle moves away from its resting position, measured from the centre to the crest or trough.
WS.hint: Think about how far up and down a particle moves from the middle.

WS.q: A loudspeaker has a cone that vibrates back and forth very quickly. What does this vibration create?
WS.explanation: When the loudspeaker cone vibrates, it pushes on the air particles around it, creating sound waves that travel outward.
WS.hint: Sound is made by vibration, and the speaker cone is vibrating.

WS.q: If the frequency of a wave increases, what happens to its wavelength (assuming the wave speed stays the same)?
WS.explanation: Frequency and wavelength are inversely proportional. If frequency increases while speed stays constant, wavelength must decrease. This is described by the equation: wave speed = frequency × wavelength.
WS.hint: If more waves fit in the same space, each wave must be shorter.

WS.q: Which of the following is an example of a sound wave?
options: The vibration of a tuning fork
WS.explanation: A tuning fork vibrates and creates sound waves. The other options do not produce sound waves.
WS.hint: Think about what creates vibrations in the air.

WS.q: What is the name of the high points on a wave diagram?
WS.explanation: The high points of a wave are called crests. The low points are called troughs.
WS.hint: Think of sea waves - what are the highest parts called?

WS.q: Which statement about sound waves is correct?
options: Sound waves can travel through a vacuum
Sound waves need a medium to travel
Sound waves travel faster in air than in water
Sound waves do not require vibration
WS.explanation: Sound waves are vibrations that travel through a medium (a substance). They cannot travel through a vacuum because there are no particles to vibrate.
WS.hint: What do sound waves need in order to propagate?

WS.q: A drum is struck with a drumstick. Which sequence correctly shows the energy transfers?
options: Light energy → Sound energy → Kinetic energy
Kinetic energy → Sound energy → Thermal energy
Kinetic energy → Sound energy → Kinetic energy of air particles
Thermal energy → Kinetic energy → Sound energy
WS.explanation: The drumstick has kinetic energy. When it strikes the drum, it creates sound energy. This sound energy causes air particles to move (kinetic energy of the particles).
WS.hint: Start with the moving drumstick and follow what happens step by step.

WS.q: What is the SI unit for measuring frequency?
WS.explanation: Frequency is measured in Hertz (Hz). It represents the number of waves passing a point per second.
WS.hint: Frequency tells us how many complete waves occur in one second.

WS.q: What do we call the number of waves that pass a point in one second?
WS.explanation: Frequency measures how many complete waves pass a point per second, measured in Hertz (Hz).
WS.hint: Think about how often waves repeat.

WS.q: A sound wave travels through air at 340 m/s. If the wavelength is 0.68 m, what is the frequency of the sound?
WS.explanation: Using the wave equation v = f × λ, we rearrange to f = v ÷ λ = 340 ÷ 0.68 = 500 Hz.
WS.hint: Use the formula: frequency = speed ÷ wavelength

WS.q: A drum is struck and produces a sound. Which of the following describes the motion of the drum head?
options: It moves in a straight line away from you
WS.explanation: When struck, the drum head oscillates (vibrates) up and down repeatedly, creating compressions and rarefactions in the air that form sound waves.
WS.hint: Think about what happens to the surface of a drum when you hit it.

WS.q: When light travels from air into glass, it slows down and bends. This is an example of…
WS.explanation: Refraction is the bending of a wave when it enters a different medium and changes speed. Diffraction is spreading; reflection is bouncing back.
WS.hint: What happens to a wave when it enters a different medium and changes speed?

WS.q: Which of the following is a transverse wave?
options: A wave on a stretched string
A compression wave in a spring
WS.explanation: A wave on a stretched string oscillates perpendicular (at right angles) to the direction of wave travel, making it transverse. Sound waves are longitudinal (parallel to travel direction).
WS.hint: Transverse waves vibrate perpendicular to the direction the wave travels.

WS.q: An oscilloscope display shows two sound waves side by side. Wave X has waves that are closer together than Wave Y. What can you conclude?
options: Wave X is louder than Wave Y
Wave X has a shorter wavelength than Wave Y
Wave X travels faster than Wave Y
Wave Y has a higher frequency than Wave X
WS.explanation: Waves that are closer together have a shorter wavelength. Wavelength depends on how the waves are spaced, not on loudness, speed (in the same medium), or frequency directly.
WS.hint: Waves closer together means the distance between peaks is smaller—that's wavelength.

WS.q: A sound wave travels through air at 340 m/s. If the wavelength of a note is 0.68 m, what is the frequency of this note?
WS.explanation: Using the equation: frequency = speed ÷ wavelength. f = 340 ÷ 0.68 = 500 Hz.
WS.hint: Use the wave equation: speed = frequency × wavelength, or rearrange to find frequency.

WS.q: Which of the following is a property of sound waves?
options: They can travel through a vacuum
They travel faster in air than in water
They do not require a medium to travel
WS.explanation: Sound waves are longitudinal waves where particles vibrate parallel to the direction of energy flow. They cannot travel through a vacuum and travel faster in denser media like water.
WS.hint: Think about whether sound needs something to travel through.

WS.q: On an oscilloscope display, which feature indicates a louder sound?
WS.explanation: Amplitude determines loudness; greater amplitude = louder sound. Frequency determines pitch, and wavelength determines the spacing of waves.
WS.hint: Loudness is related to how much energy the wave carries, shown by wave height.

WS.q: A tuning fork vibrates at 256 Hz. This means the fork completes one full vibration in…
WS.explanation: If frequency is 256 Hz (256 vibrations per second), then one vibration takes 1 ÷ 256 seconds. This is the period.
WS.hint: Period = 1 ÷ frequency. How long does one complete cycle take?

WS.q: Sound travels faster in water than in air. Compared to sound in air, sound in water has…
options: A lower frequency and longer wavelength
A higher frequency and shorter wavelength
The same frequency but a longer wavelength
The same frequency but a shorter wavelength
WS.explanation: The frequency of sound does not change when it moves between media (it is determined by the source). However, wavelength = speed ÷ frequency, so when speed increases and frequency stays the same, wavelength increases. Wait—the answer is the same frequency but LONGER wavelength. Correction: Option 3 states 'shorter' which is incorrect. The correct reasoning is: same frequency, longer wavelength (since speed increases). Please note: There is an error in this question's options.
WS.hint: Frequency is set by the source and doesn't change between media. Use: wavelength = speed ÷ frequency.

AE.q: What is the smallest particle of an element that still has the properties of that element?
AE.explanation: An atom is the smallest particle of an element that retains the element's properties.
AE.hint: Think about the tiniest piece of something.

AE.q: Which of the following is NOT an element?
AE.explanation: Salt is a compound made of sodium and chlorine atoms bonded together, not an element.
AE.hint: Which one is made from two different elements?

AE.q: What does the periodic table show?
options: All the compounds in the world
AE.explanation: The periodic table is an organized display of all the known elements.
AE.hint: It's a table that chemists use.

AE.q: What is the chemical symbol for gold?
AE.explanation: Gold's symbol is Au, which comes from its Latin name 'aurum'.
AE.hint: The symbol comes from an old language name for gold.

AE.q: Which statement about atoms is correct?
options: Atoms can be seen with the naked eye
Atoms are made up of smaller particles
All atoms are the same size
Atoms cannot combine with other atoms
AE.explanation: Atoms are made up of smaller particles like protons, neutrons, and electrons.
AE.hint: Think about what atoms are made from.

AE.q: What does the number after an element's symbol tell you?
options: How many atoms are bonded together
AE.explanation: The number after an element's symbol shows how many atoms are bonded together. For example, O₂ means two oxygen atoms bonded.
AE.hint: Look at examples like H₂O or O₂.

AE.q: Which of these is a mixture?
AE.explanation: A sugar solution is a mixture because sugar and water are mixed together but not chemically bonded.
AE.hint: Which one has two substances that can be separated?

AE.q: What is the smallest particle of an element called?
AE.explanation: An atom is the smallest particle of an element that still has the properties of that element.
AE.hint: Think about what the smallest unit is...

AE.q: Which of the following is an element?
AE.explanation: Carbon is made of only one type of atom (carbon atoms), so it is an element. The others are compounds or mixtures.
AE.hint: An element contains only one type of atom.

AE.q: What is the chemical symbol for oxygen?
AE.explanation: The chemical symbol for oxygen is O (a single capital letter). O₂ is a molecule of oxygen gas with two atoms.
AE.hint: Chemical symbols are usually one or two letters.

AE.q: Which statement about the periodic table is true?
AE.explanation: The periodic table is an organized list of all known elements arranged by their properties.
AE.hint: Think about what the periodic table organizes.

AE.q: What is the chemical symbol for nitrogen?
AE.explanation: The chemical symbol for nitrogen is N. Ni is nickel, No is nobelium, and Nr is not a real symbol.
AE.hint: Nitrogen is a gas in the air we breathe. Its symbol is a single capital letter.

AE.q: What is the total number of atoms in one molecule of calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)?
AE.explanation: Calcium carbonate contains: 1 calcium atom + 1 carbon atom + 3 oxygen atoms = 5 atoms total.
AE.hint: Count Ca(1) + C(1) + O(3) carefully.

AE.q: Which statement about the periodic table is correct?
options: Elements are arranged by colour from left to right
Elements in the same period have similar chemical properties
Elements in the same group have similar chemical properties
All elements in row 2 are gases
AE.explanation: Elements in the same group (vertical column) have similar chemical properties because they have the same number of outer shell electrons.
AE.hint: Remember: groups are columns, periods are rows.

AE.q: Which of the following is a compound?
AE.explanation: Carbon dioxide (CO₂) is made of two different elements chemically bonded together, making it a compound. The others are elements.
AE.hint: A compound must contain at least two different elements joined together.

AE.q: What is the atomic number of an element?
options: The total number of protons and neutrons
The number of protons in an atom
The number of neutrons in an atom
The mass of the atom in grams
AE.explanation: The atomic number is the number of protons in an atom of an element. This number identifies which element it is.
AE.hint: The atomic number tells you the identity of the element.

AE.q: Which element is in Group 2 and Period 3 of the periodic table?
AE.explanation: Magnesium (Mg) is located in Group 2 (vertical column) and Period 3 (horizontal row). Sodium is in Period 3 but Group 1.
AE.hint: Find the intersection of Group 2 (column) and Period 3 (row).

AE.q: What is the total number of atoms in two molecules of CH₄?
AE.explanation: One CH₄ molecule contains 1 carbon + 4 hydrogen = 5 atoms. Two molecules contain 5 × 2 = 10 atoms.
AE.hint: Count atoms in one molecule first, then multiply by 2.

AE.q: Which statement about atoms is correct?
options: Atoms are made of smaller particles called molecules
Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus
All atoms have the same number of electrons
AE.explanation: The nucleus contains protons (positive charge) and neutrons (neutral). Electrons orbit around the nucleus in electron shells.
AE.hint: Think about the structure of an atom and what is at the centre.

AE.q: What is the chemical formula for a molecule containing 2 nitrogen atoms and 5 oxygen atoms?
AE.explanation: The subscript numbers show how many atoms of each element are present. 2 nitrogen atoms and 5 oxygen atoms is written as N₂O₅.
AE.hint: The number after each element symbol shows how many atoms of that element are in the molecule.

AE.q: Which of the following correctly describes the arrangement of electrons in atoms?
options: Electrons are randomly scattered throughout the atom
Electrons are found only in the nucleus
Electrons occupy shells around the nucleus at specific energy levels
Electrons are always on the surface of the atom
AE.explanation: Electrons orbit the nucleus in shells (or energy levels). Each shell can hold a maximum number of electrons. The first shell holds max 2, the second holds max 8.
AE.hint: Think about the structure of the atom and how electrons are arranged around the nucleus.

AE.q: Which pair of elements would you expect to have the most similar chemical properties?
AE.explanation: Chlorine and bromine are both in Group 17 (halogens) of the periodic table. They have similar electron arrangements in their outer shells, making their properties similar.
AE.hint: Look at which elements are in the same group (column) of the periodic table.

AE.q: Magnesium has an atomic number of 12 and a mass number of 24. How many neutrons are in a magnesium atom?
AE.explanation: Neutrons = mass number − atomic number = 24 − 12 = 12. Wait, the correct answer is 12, so the index should be 0. Let me recalculate: mass number (24) minus atomic number (12) equals 12 neutrons.
AE.hint: Use the formula: neutrons = mass number − atomic number

AE.q: When sodium and chlorine react to form sodium chloride, electrons are transferred from sodium to chlorine. What type of bonding occurs in the product?
AE.explanation: When electrons are transferred from one atom to another, ions are formed. The electrostatic attraction between these oppositely charged ions is called ionic bonding.
AE.hint: What happens when electrons move from one atom to another?

AE.q: Which statement correctly describes the relationship between atomic number and mass number?
options: Atomic number is the number of protons; mass number is the total of protons and neutrons
Mass number is always twice the atomic number
Atomic number determines the charge of the nucleus only
AE.explanation: Atomic number (Z) = number of protons. Mass number (A) = number of protons + number of neutrons. This is the standard nuclear notation foundation.
AE.hint: What does each number in nuclear notation represent?

AE.q: In the periodic table, why do elements in Group 1 (alkali metals) have similar chemical properties?
options: They have the same number of neutrons
They have the same number of electrons in their outermost shell
They have the same atomic mass
They all form the same compounds
AE.explanation: Elements in the same group have the same number of electrons in their outermost shell, which determines their chemical behavior and reactivity patterns.
AE.hint: What feature of an atom determines how it reacts with other elements?

AE.q: An element has the electron configuration 2,8,7. Which of the following statements is true?
options: It is an alkali metal with atomic number 17
It is a halogen with atomic number 17
It is a transition metal with atomic number 17
It is a noble gas with atomic number 18
AE.explanation: An element with 2,8,7 electrons has 17 protons (atomic number 17), making it chlorine. With 7 electrons in the outermost shell, it is in Group 17 (halogens), not a noble gas.
AE.hint: Count the total electrons to find the atomic number. What group has 7 outer electrons?

AE.q: An atom of nitrogen has 7 protons and 7 neutrons. What is its mass number?
AE.explanation: Mass number = protons + neutrons = 7 + 7 = 14
AE.hint: Mass number counts both protons and neutrons in the nucleus.

AE.q: Which statement correctly explains why sodium and chlorine react to form sodium chloride?
options: Sodium and chlorine atoms are identical in size
Sodium atoms transfer electrons to chlorine atoms, forming an ionic compound
AE.explanation: Sodium is a reactive metal that loses electrons to chlorine, a reactive non-metal, forming ionic bonds and the compound NaCl.
AE.hint: Think about what reactive metals and non-metals do during bonding.

AE.q: A sample of pure copper has a mass of 64 g. How many moles of copper are present? (Relative atomic mass of Cu = 64)
AE.explanation: Number of moles = mass ÷ relative atomic mass = 64 ÷ 64 = 1 mole
AE.hint: Use the formula: moles = mass ÷ Ar
